text:
Cheers for this Rob, thank you so much for your enthusiasm, and for explaining this in such a way that even me hubby will be able to understand,

You'd have been an amazing person to teach people 
 (* I am not the best of students..)

The Dynopod is shaping up beautifully,,
I am still collecting parts, so that I may soon be ready to go off - grid, in a city bedsit. 

although I have to remain connected, otherwise my landlord will be pretty annoyed with me!! Lol!!

Best Wishes, and Kind Regards, Andrea, Jasper and George the Pigeon, and Mr Dingbatt too. XxX.

author: @McRootbeer
text: It would be neat to see a diy adsorption refrigerator or air conditioner powered by a pedal powered vacuum pump.  Pretty sure that combines 3 of your previous projects.  Though I doubt you have the parts lying around after all this time. Also, do you think an adsorption chillers hot side could provide enough of a temp difference to run a low temp stirling engine?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a really neat idea mate - after I have finished the paper bike iw ill get on with that I think

replies.author: @McRootbeer
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering awesome, it's be neat to see how different materials would affect performance.  And if you could talk a bit more on the possibility of a solid state stirling engine while you're interested.  I think I get the idea,(doing work by having a material change from liquid to gas based on temp and pressure differences)  but I can't think how you'd get the power out.

author: @chuxxsss
text: Your welding is getting better Rob.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Hai! Your welding IS certainly improving. ;-)  And do I hear an 'inverter' welder?
One tip, if I may, angle the stick up in the clamp 45deg, it's far easier to control the arc that way, and the feedback feel is much better. and your hand is less likely to get in your view.
Nice looking project.
Thanks for sharing.

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Bloody rippa mate, I love the gears and derailer system, it really takes it up a notch (and down, lol).
When you pulled out that flywheel I realised that you could use a large heavy weightlifting weight as a flywheel like you have here but larger and heavier. They are usually well balanced and ready to weld on the cog.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
Energy stored in a flywheel goes with the square of rpm, so logically a much higher final input gear ratio and a smaller/faster flywheel would yield same/more power for less mass. Of course at higher rpm air/friction losses might start to come into it a bit. And I don't think I'd want anything much over a 10kg disc fizzing round under my chin, but I'm over-cautious/chicken.  ;-/ And don't dismiss the gyroscopic effects too glibly, I once left a flywheel on test, and came back several hours later to find it canted over about 30-deg.
You are very unlikely to 'pop' one, but I wonder what the burst rpm is of a weight-lifting 25kg disc. And what the energy stored would be at, 'She canny take any more, cap'n'!   ;-O    D'ya wanna try it? (safely, of course). I think I'd try it in a deep hole in the ground, just in case.

author: @milliwaysgarage_ie
text: Great Idea, I wonder how long the freewheel gearing will last at high speeds over time. Now all there's left is springy seat with small damper for pleasant seat on bumpy roads.

author: @cheerdiver
text:
Consider that many invalids are over weight, due to lack of exercise.  Legs provide a majority of muscle used in cycling.  Modern delta/tad pole trikes are too light to support a 400+lb person.
In addition, the drive train wouldn't be substantial enough for the load.
Heavy duty exercise trike, that using hydraulics.  The main benefit being fluid control of input resistance.  Accumulators would serve the same function as a flywheel.
New experience with spear valves.  Use the frame for plumbing/storage.

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: Wow, let us know how that turns out after you build it
